title: Headings
content:
# Headings

Headings are used to structure your document into sections.

They make your content easier to read, navigate, and organize.

---

## Syntax

A heading is created using the `#` symbol followed by a space, then your text.

The number of `#` symbols determines the level of the heading.

```markdown
# Heading 1
## Heading 2
### Heading 3
#### Heading 4
##### Heading 5
###### Heading 6
```

---

## How It Works

- `#` = largest heading (main title)
- `######` = smallest heading
- You can use up to 6 levels

---

## Rules

- `#` must be at the **start of the line**
- A **space is required** after the `#`
- Text must come **after the space**

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
#Heading
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
# Heading
```

---

## Example

# Heading 1
## Heading 2
### Heading 3
#### Heading 4

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
## My Section Title
```

Then press Enter and continue writing your content below it.

title: Bold & Italic
content:
# Bold & Italic

Bold and italic formatting are used to emphasize text.

They help highlight important words, phrases, or ideas.

---

## Syntax

You must wrap text with matching symbols on both sides.

```markdown
*italic*
**bold**
***bold italic***
```

---

## How It Works

- `*text*` → *Italic*
- `**text**` → **Bold**
- `***text***` → ***Bold Italic***

---

## Rules

- Formatting symbols must **wrap the text completely**
- Opening and closing symbols must **match exactly**
- No space between the symbols and the text

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
* italic *
**bold
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
*italic*
**bold**
```

---

## Example

This is *italic text*.

This is **bold text**.

This is ***bold and italic text***.

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
This is *important* and this is **very important**.
```

Then experiment by combining them:

```markdown
***Very important text***
```

title: Inline Code
content:
# Inline Code

Inline code is used to highlight short pieces of code, commands, or technical terms within a sentence.

It is commonly used for things like commands, variable names, or file paths.

---

## Syntax

Wrap the text with a single backtick (`) on both sides.

```markdown
`code`
```

---

## How It Works

- Text inside backticks is displayed in a monospace font
- Formatting like *italic* or **bold** will NOT be applied inside inline code

Example:

Use `npm install` to install packages.

---

## Rules

- Must start and end with a backtick (`)
- No spaces between the backtick and the text

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
`
 code 
`
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
`code`
```

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
Run `npm start` to launch the app.
```

title: Lists
content:
# Lists

Lists help organize content into clear, readable items.

You can create unordered (bullet) lists and ordered (numbered) lists.

---

## Bullet List (Unordered)

## Syntax

Use `-`, `*`, or `+` followed by a space.

```markdown
- Item one
- Item two
- Item three
```

---

## Numbered List (Ordered)

## Syntax

Use a number followed by a period and a space.

```markdown
1. First
2. Second
3. Third
```

---

## Nested Lists

You can create sub-items by indenting with spaces.

```markdown
- Item one
- Item two
  - Nested item
  - Another nested item
- Item three
```

---

## Rules

- A space is required after `-` or `1.`
- Indentation must be consistent (use 2 spaces)
- Mixing list types is allowed but should be structured clearly

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
-Item
1.First
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
- Item
1. First
```

---

## Example

- Fruits
  - Apple
  - Banana
- Vegetables
  - Carrot

1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
- Task 1
- Task 2
  - Subtask 2.1
```

Then try creating your own numbered steps.

title: Tables
content:
# Tables

Tables are used to display structured data in rows and columns.

They are especially useful for comparing values or organizing information clearly.

---

## Syntax

A table is created using pipes (`|`) to separate columns.

The second row is required and defines the column structure.

```markdown
| Column 1 | Column 2 |
|----------|----------|
| Value A  | Value B  |
```

---

## How It Works

- First row = column headers
- Second row = separator (must use dashes `-`)
- Remaining rows = data

---

## Alignment (Optional)

You can control text alignment using colons (`:`).

```markdown
| Left | Center | Right |
|:-----|:------:|------:|
| A    | B      | C     |
```

- `:---` = left aligned
- `:---:` = centered
- `---:` = right aligned

---

## Rules

- Pipes (`|`) must separate each column
- The separator row is required
- Each row should have the same number of columns

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
| Name | Status |
| Name | Active |
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
| Name | Status |
|------|--------|
| Demo | Active |
```

---

## Example

| Name | Status |
|------|--------|
| Demo | Active |
| Test | Pending |

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
| Task | Done |
|------|------|
| Learn | Yes |
| Build | No  |
```

Then try adding another column.

title: Blockquotes
content:
# Blockquotes

Blockquotes are used to highlight quoted text, notes, or important sections.

They are commonly used for quotes, tips, or emphasis.

---

## Syntax

Start a line with a greater-than symbol (`>`) followed by a space.

```markdown
> This is a quote
```

---

## How It Works

- Everything after `>` becomes part of the quote
- You can span multiple lines by repeating `>`

```markdown
> This is a quote
> that continues on another line
```

---

## Nested Blockquotes

You can nest blockquotes by adding more `>` symbols.

```markdown
> Outer quote
>> Inner quote
```

---

## Rules

- `>` must be at the start of the line
- A space is required after `>`

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
>This is wrong
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
> This is correct
```

---

## Example

> This is a quote.
>
> It can span multiple lines.

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
> This is an important note
```

Then try creating a nested quote:

```markdown
> Main idea
>> Supporting detail
```

title: Callouts
content:
# Callouts

Callouts are used to highlight important information such as notes, warnings, or tips.

They are an extension of blockquotes with a structured format.

---

## Syntax

A callout starts with a blockquote (`>`) followed by a type label.

```markdown
> [!type] Title
> Content goes here
```

---

## How It Works

- `>` creates the blockquote container
- `[!type]` defines the callout style
- The title appears next to the type
- Additional lines must also start with `>`

---

## Common Types

```markdown
> [!info] Info
> Informational message

> [!warning] Warning
> Something to be careful about

> [!tip] Tip
> Helpful suggestion

> [!note] Note
> General note
```

---

## Rules

- Must start with `>`
- `[!type]` must be inside square brackets
- A space is required after `>`
- Every line in the callout must begin with `>`

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
[!info] Missing blockquote
> [!info]Missing space
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
> [!info] Info
> Proper callout format
```

---

## Example

> [!info] Info
> This is an informational message.

> [!warning] Warning
> Be careful with this action.

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
> [!tip] Tip
> Try experimenting with different types.
```

Then create your own custom callout.

title: Horizontal Rules
content:
# Horizontal Rules

Separate sections visually.

## Example

---

## Try it
Use `---` or `***`.

title: Task Lists
content:
# Task Lists

Task lists are used to track progress with checkboxes.

They are useful for to-do lists, workflows, and planning.

---

## Syntax

A task list is created using a list (`-`) followed by a checkbox.

```markdown
- [ ] Not done
- [x] Done
```

---

## How It Works

- `[ ]` = unchecked task
- `[x]` = completed task
- Must be part of a list (`-` or `*`)

---

## Rules

- A space is required inside the brackets
- A space is required after the closing bracket
- Must follow list syntax

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
-[ ]Task
- [x]Done
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
- [ ] Task
- [x] Done
```

---

## Nested Tasks

You can create subtasks using indentation.

```markdown
- [ ] Main task
  - [ ] Subtask
  - [x] Completed subtask
```

---

## Example

- [x] Learn markdown
- [ ] Build a project
  - [ ] Setup files
  - [ ] Write content

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
- [ ] Task one
- [x] Task two
```

Then try adding subtasks under one item.

title: Links
content:
# Links

Links allow you to connect to external websites or other notes inside your workspace.

---

## External Links

External links point to websites using a URL.

### Syntax

```markdown
[text](https://example.com)
```

### Example

[Open Example](https://example.com)

---

## Internal Links

Internal links connect to other files in your project.

### Syntax

```markdown
[[/path/to/file]]
```

### Example

```markdown
[[/Formatting Examples/Blockquotes]]
```

This will link to the **Blockquotes** file.

---

## How It Works

- External links use `[text](url)`
- Internal links use `[[path]]`
- Internal paths must match the file path exactly

---

## Rules

- External links require both `[]` and `()`
- Internal links require double brackets `[[ ]]`
- Paths are case-sensitive and must be exact

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
[Example]https://example.com
[[Formatting Examples/Blockqoutes]]
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
[Example](https://example.com)
[[/Formatting Examples/Blockquotes]]
```

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
[Visit site](https://example.com)

[[/Formatting Examples/Headings]]
```

Then try linking to another file in your workspace.

title: Tags
content:
# Tags

Tag your content for organization.

## Example

#productivity #notes

## Try it
Use `#tag`.

title: Strikethrough & Highlight
content:
# Strikethrough & Highlight

Strikethrough and highlight are used to modify how text appears for emphasis or clarity.

They are useful for showing removed content or drawing attention to important text.

---

## Syntax

```markdown
~~text~~
==text==
```

---

## How It Works

- `~~text~~` → ~~Strikethrough~~ (used to show removed or outdated content)
- `==text==` → ==Highlight== (used to emphasize important content)

---

## Rules

- Symbols must wrap the text completely
- Opening and closing symbols must match
- No spaces between symbols and text

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
~~ text ~~
==highlight
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
~~text~~
==highlight==
```

---

## Combining Formatting

You can combine with other formatting like bold or italic.

```markdown
**~~bold removed~~**
==*highlighted italic*==
```

---

## Example

This is ~~deleted text~~.

This is ==important text==.

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
This is ~~old~~ and this is ==new==.
```

Then try combining it with bold or italic formatting.

title: Internal Links
content:
# Internal Links

Internal links connect one note to another within your workspace.

They allow you to build a network of related content.

---

## Syntax

```markdown
[[/path/to/file]]
```

---

## How It Works

- The path must match the file path exactly
- When clicked, it opens the linked file
- This creates a connection between notes

---

## Linking to Existing Files

```markdown
[[/Welcome to Your project]]
```

```markdown
[[/Formatting Examples/Blockquotes]]
```

---

## Rules

- Must use double brackets `[[ ]]`
- Paths are **case-sensitive**
- Paths must be exact (including spaces)
- Always include the leading `/`

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
[[Formatting Examples/Blockquotes]]
[[/formatting examples/blockquotes]]
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
[[/Formatting Examples/Blockquotes]]
```

---

## Notes

- If the path does not exist, the link may break or not resolve
- Use consistent naming for files to avoid errors

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
[[/Formatting Examples/Headings]]
```

Then try linking to another file in your workspace.

title: Frontmatter
content:
# Frontmatter

Frontmatter lets you add metadata to your note.

## Important
Frontmatter only works if it starts at the very first line of the file.

If the document does not begin with `---`, it will not be treated as frontmatter.

## Example

---
title: Sample
tags: demo, test
---

## Invalid Example

Text before frontmatter

---
title: This will not work
---

## Try it
Start your note with `---`, add your fields, then close with `---`.

title: Code Blocks
content:
# Code Blocks

Code blocks are used to display larger pieces of code or preformatted text.

They preserve spacing, formatting, and optionally enable syntax highlighting.

---

## Syntax

Wrap your code with triple backticks (```).

```markdown
```language
code here
```
```

---

## How It Works

- Opening backticks start the block
- Closing backticks end the block
- You can specify a language for syntax highlighting

---

## With Language (Recommended)

```markdown
```javascript
console.log("Hello world");
```
```

---

## Without Language

```markdown
```
plain text
```
```

---

## Rules

- Must start and end with **exactly three backticks**
- Opening and closing backticks must match
- The closing backticks must be on a new line

❌ Incorrect:
```markdown
```js
console.log("Hello")
``
```

✅ Correct:
```markdown
```js
console.log("Hello")
```
```

---

## Nested Code Blocks

If your code contains triple backticks, use four backticks to wrap the outer block.

````markdown
````
```javascript
console.log("Nested block");
```
````
````

---

## Example

```javascript
function greet() {
  console.log("Hello world");
}
```

---

## Try it

Type this exactly:

```markdown
```javascript
const x = 10;
console.log(x);
```
```

Then try changing the language (e.g., `json`, `bash`, `html`).

title: Example Content
content:
# Example Content

## Overview

This document demonstrates how markdown content is rendered in the editor, including support for code fences, syntax highlighting, and common formatting features.

It is intended as a visual showcase to verify how different markdown elements appear, rather than as formal documentation.

## What This Demo Covers

The examples below highlight:

- Code fences with syntax highlighting
- Inline code formatting
- Headings and text structure
- Lists and nesting
- Links and formatting
- Tables and extended markdown features

Use this page to test and preview how markdown is displayed in the system.

## Headings

```yaml
description: |
  # Heading 1
  ## Heading 2
  ### Heading 3
```

## Paragraphs and Line Breaks

```yaml
description: |
  This is a paragraph of text.

  This is another paragraph. Two newlines create a paragraph break.

  Use two spaces at the end of a line  
  to create a hard line break within a paragraph.
```

## Emphasis

```yaml
description: |
  *Italic text* or _italic text_

  **Bold text** or __bold text__

  ***Bold and italic*** or ___bold and italic___
```

## Lists

```yaml
description: |
  Unordered list:
  - Item one
  - Item two
    - Nested item
  - Item three

  Ordered list:
  1. First item
  2. Second item
  3. Third item
```

## Links

```yaml
description: |
  [Link text](https://example.com)

  [Link with title](https://example.com "Link title")

  Autolink: <https://example.com>
```

## Code

```yaml
description: |
  Inline `code` in text.

  Code block:
  ```json
  {
    "key": "value"
  }
  ```

  Indented code block:
      function example() {
        return true;
      }
```

## Blockquotes

```yaml
description: |
  > This is a blockquote.
  >
  > It can span multiple lines.

  > Nested blockquotes:
  > > Inner quote
```

## Horizontal Rules

```yaml
description: |
  Content above

  ---

  Content below
```

## Tables (GFM Extension)

```yaml
description: |
  | Column 1 | Column 2 | Column 3 |
  |----------|----------|----------|
  | Row 1    | Data     | Data     |
  | Row 2    | Data     | Data     |
```

## Task Lists (GFM Extension)

```yaml
description: |
  - [x] Completed task
  - [ ] Incomplete task
  - [ ] Another task
```

## Example Usage

```yaml
info:
  title: Demo API
  version: 1.0.0
  description: |
    # Demo API

    This section demonstrates a realistic markdown usage scenario.

    ## Features

    - **Example Data**
    - **Formatted Output**
    - **Syntax Highlighting**

    ## Example Request

    ```bash
    curl -X GET "https://api.example.com/data" \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer token"
    ```
```


## Best Practices

1. **Use headings for structure** — Break long descriptions into sections
2. **Include code examples** — Show request/response examples
3. **Use tables for lists of values** — Enums, status codes, parameters
4. **Add callouts for important info** — Use blockquotes for warnings/notes
5. **Link to external docs** — Reference detailed documentation

title: Welcome to Your Project
content:
---
tags: home, welcome
---

# Welcome to Your Project 👋

This is your workspace for writing, organizing, and connecting ideas.

Everything here is powered by markdown — simple, flexible, and powerful.

---

## What You Can Do

- Write structured notes using headings and formatting
- Organize ideas with lists, tables, and tasks
- Add code snippets and diagrams
- Link notes together to build a knowledge system

---

## Start Writing

Learn how formatting works by exploring examples:

- [[/Formatting Examples/Headings]] — structure your content
- [[/Formatting Examples/Bold & Italic]] — emphasize text
- [[/Formatting Examples/Lists]] — organize ideas
- [[/Formatting Examples/Code Blocks]] — write code
- [[/Formatting Examples/Tables]] — display structured data
- [[/Formatting Examples/Mermaid Diagrams]] — create diagrams

---

## Connect Your Notes

Link your notes together to build relationships between ideas:

- [[/Formatting Examples/Internal Links]] — link between notes
- [[/Formatting Examples/Links]] — external and internal links

---

## Work Efficiently

Use built-in tools to edit faster and smarter:

- [[/Context Menu]] — quick formatting and insert tools
- [[/Editor Options]] — switch between editing modes

---

## Customize Your Workspace

Make the editor feel like your own:

- [[/Settings/Settings]] — overview of settings
- [[/Settings/Appearance]] — themes, fonts, and layout

---

## Example

Here’s a simple note you can try:

```markdown
# My First Note

## Tasks
- [ ] Learn markdown
- [ ] Build something

## Idea
This is ==important== and this is *emphasized*.

## Code
    ```javascript
    console.log("Hello world");
    ```
```

---

## Tips

- Start small — write a few lines and expand
- Use formatting to make content readable
- Link notes to build a connected system

---

You're ready. Start writing 🚀

title: Context Menu
content:
# Context Menu

The context menu gives you quick access to formatting and editing tools directly inside the editor.

## How to open

Right-click anywhere inside the editor to open the menu.

The available options depend on where your cursor is placed.

## Menu types

### General menu

This appears when working with normal text.

It allows you to:

- Format text (bold, italic, highlight, etc.)
- Create lists and headings
- Insert elements like tables, callouts, or code blocks
- Add internal and external links

### Callout menu

This appears when your cursor is inside a callout block.

It allows you to:

- Edit the callout content
- Change the callout type (info, warning, tip, etc.)
- Remove the callout

## Formatting options

From the **Format** section, you can apply:

- **Bold**, *Italic*, ~~Strikethrough~~, ==Highlight==
- Inline `code` and math `$E=mc^2$`
- Comments using `%%hidden text%%`

## Paragraph options

From the **Paragraph** section:

- Bullet, numbered, and task lists
- Headings (1–6)
- Quotes

## Insert options

From the **Insert** section:

- Tables
- Callouts
- Horizontal rules
- Code blocks
- Math blocks

## Try it

1. Write some text
2. Right-click inside the editor
3. Choose a formatting option like **Bold**
4. Try switching to a callout and right-click again

## Notes

- The menu changes based on your current position
- Some options only appear in specific contexts
- Formatting can be combined for more complex content

To see how each feature works in detail, explore [[/Formatting Examples]].

title: Settings
content:
# Settings

Settings allow you to customize how the editor looks and behaves.

## Where to find it

You can open Settings from the left sidebar.

- Look at the bottom section of the sidebar
- Click the **Settings** icon

This will open the settings panel.

## What you can configure

Settings are grouped into sections such as:

- Appearance
- Editor behavior
- Files and links
- Plugins

## Start here

To begin customizing your workspace, open:

- [[/Settings/Appearance]]

## Notes

- Changes are applied immediately
- Some options affect only the editor
- Others change the overall interface

title: Appearance
content:
# Appearance

The Appearance settings let you customize how your workspace looks.

## Theme

Choose how the interface is displayed:

- Light
- Dark
- System (follows your device settings)

## Accent Color

Change the highlight color used in the editor.

This affects:
- Cursor color
- Selection highlights

## Layout

Control how elements are displayed:

- Toggle inline title
- Show or hide the tab title bar

## Typography

Customize how text appears:

- Interface font (menus and panels)
- Document font (your notes)
- Monospace font (code and technical text)
- Font size

## Try it

1. Open Settings
2. Go to Appearance
3. Change the theme or font size
4. Observe the changes in real time

## Notes

- Changes are applied instantly
- Some fonts depend on what is available on your system
- Reset options are available for certain settings

title: Editor Options
content:
# Editor Options

Editor Options let you control how a file is displayed and managed.

## How to open

Right-click on a tab at the top of the editor.

This will open the tab menu.

## View modes

### Reading View

Switch between editable content and a clean reading layout.

- Focus on content without editing distractions

### Source Mode

View the raw markdown source of your note.

- See the underlying formatting symbols

### Chunk Mode

Split the document into editable sections.

- Useful for working with large notes
- Available only in supported files

## Tab management

You can manage open tabs directly from the menu:

- Close Tab
- Close Others
- Close Tabs to the Right

## Try it

1. Open multiple notes
2. Right-click on a tab
3. Try switching to Reading View or Source Mode
4. Close tabs using the menu options

## Notes

- Some options may not appear depending on the file
- View modes only affect the current tab
- Changes do not modify your content

To learn how to edit content, see [[/Context Menu]].
